name: Marcus Johnson
chief_complaint: My leg is killing me. The pain is way worse than it should be for the fracture. Something feels really wrong.
onset_description: Sustained a closed tibial shaft fracture of his right leg in a motorcycle accident 8 hours ago. Was splinted in the ED and admitted for observation. Pain has been escalating despite IV pain medication and is now severe, constant, and out of proportion to injury. Nursing staff called the physician because patient is in extreme distress.

severe_leg_pain.description: This is not normal fracture pain. I've broken bones before. Something is seriously wrong. It feels like my leg is going to explode. The pressure is unbearable.
severe_leg_pain.onset: Pain escalating over past 4-6 hours despite fracture being splinted
severe_leg_pain.character: deep, unrelenting, pressure-like pain out of proportion to injury. Not relieved by immobilization or IV opioids.
alleviating_factors: IV morphine 4mg given x3 with minimal effect

leg_tightness_and_swelling.description: My whole lower leg feels rock hard and swollen. It's way bigger than the other side. The skin feels like it's going to split.
leg_tightness_and_swelling.character: tense, woody-hard anterior compartment. Circumference significantly greater than contralateral leg.
leg_tightness_and_swelling.location: right lower leg, anterior and lateral compartments

foot_numbness.description: I'm losing feeling in my foot. The space between my big toe and second toe is numb. I'm scared I'm going to lose my leg.
foot_numbness.character: numbness in first web space (deep peroneal nerve distribution), progressing
foot_numbness.location: right foot, first web space and dorsum of foot

inability_to_move_toes.description: I can't pull my toes up anymore. I could move them fine earlier but now they barely move.
inability_to_move_toes.character: progressive weakness of toe and ankle dorsiflexion (anterior compartment muscles)

anxiety_and_distress.description: Something is really wrong and nobody is listening to me. I keep telling the nurses the pain meds aren't working and something is different. Please help me.
anxiety_and_distress.character: extreme anxiety and agitation from uncontrolled pain and fear of limb loss

symptoms_absent: absence of distal pulses (pulses still present but diminished)
head injury or loss of consciousness during accident

labs.CBC: WBC 12.4 (mildly elevated, stress response), Hgb 13.8, Plt 256
labs.BMP: Na 139, K 4.8, Cr 1.0, BUN 14, Glucose 132 (stress response)
labs.compartment_pressure: Right anterior compartment: 48 mmHg (critically elevated; diastolic BP 94, delta pressure = 46 mmHg, threshold for fasciotomy is delta <30 mmHg)
labs.xray_right_tibia: Transverse midshaft tibial fracture with minimal displacement, intact fibula. No gas in soft tissues.

past_medical_history: Right forearm fracture from skateboarding at age 14 (healed without complication)
medications: Ibuprofen as needed for occasional headaches

social_history.drugs: Occasional marijuana use (last use 1 week ago)
social_history.living_situation: Lives with girlfriend in a second-floor apartment (stairs). No elevator.
social_history.exercise: Very active, works out 6 days a week. Weightlifting, running, and cycling. Rides a motorcycle for recreation.
social_history.diet: High protein diet, meal preps. Takes protein supplements.

conditions.paternal_grandfather: Type 2 Diabetes, died at 70 from stroke

personality: Normally confident and assertive. Currently panicked and vocal about his distress. Not a complainer â€” the fact that he is in this much distress is itself a clinical sign. Becomes frustrated and scared when he feels his concerns are being minimized. Desperate for someone to take action.
communication_style: Direct, loud, and urgent. Not trying to be difficult â€” genuinely terrified. Uses emphatic language to convey severity. Repeats that 'something is wrong' and 'this is different from the fracture pain.' Cooperative when he feels heard and a plan is being made. Uses profanity from pain.
pain_description_style: Describes pain with urgency and intensity. Uses words like 'exploding,' 'bursting,' 'pressure.' Clearly distinguishes this pain from his prior fracture experience. Says the pain medication 'isn't touching it.' Physically cannot stay still â€” writhing, gripping bedrails, sweating.

critical_findings: Compartment syndrome: the 5 P's â€” Pain out of proportion, Pain with passive stretch, Pressure (tense compartment), Paresthesias, Paralysis (progressive motor deficit)
Compartment pressure 48 mmHg with delta pressure of 46 mmHg (diastolic 94 - compartment 48 = 46; normal delta >30, his is critically close)
Progressive neurological deficit: loss of dorsiflexion and first web space sensation (deep peroneal nerve compromise)
Elevated CK (2,840) indicating ongoing muscle necrosis/rhabdomyolysis
Pain refractory to IV opioids â€” hallmark of compartment syndrome
Time-critical emergency: irreversible muscle death occurs within 6-8 hours of onset
Note: presence of distal pulses does NOT rule out compartment syndrome â€” pulses are often preserved until very late
rubric.diagnosis: Acute compartment syndrome of the right lower leg (anterior compartment)
recommended_interventions: EMERGENT orthopedic surgery consult for fasciotomy â€” do NOT delay
Four-compartment fasciotomy of the right lower leg
Remove all constrictive dressings and splints immediately
Keep limb at heart level (do NOT elevate â€” reduces perfusion pressure)
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to prevent renal damage from myoglobinuria
Monitor urine output (Foley catheter, target >1 mL/kg/hr)
Serial CK and renal function monitoring for rhabdomyolysis
Pain management (will improve significantly after fasciotomy)
Monitor for hyperkalemia from muscle breakdown (K+ 4.8 already trending up)
Serial neurovascular checks every 30 minutes until surgery
Document timing of symptom onset for medicolegal purposes
Consider alkalinization of urine with IV bicarbonate if myoglobinuria develops
